boxes.text: Over 39 days you ran 151 sessions generating 31,915 events across 26 distinct projects. Two projects dominate: route-optimization-nvidia-cuopt (81 sessions) and ai-code-review (38 sessions) — together 79% of all sessions. You're a claude-opus-4-8 house (89.4% of turns), and Friday+Saturday absorb 48% of events, pointing to a weekend-heavy cadence. Bash and Edit are the workhorses at 2,185 and 2,072 calls respectively.

boxes.note: Overview from signals.usage.overview — 39-day window (2026-05-20 to 2026-06-28), claude agent only

boxes.title: Top Tools
boxes.note: Global tool frequency from signals.usage.tools.global_frequency — top 8 of 18 distinct tools

boxes.note: From signals.usage.models — assistant message counts per model; opus-4-8 is 89.4% of all turns

boxes.title: Events by Day of Week
boxes.note: From signals.usage.temporal.by_day_of_week — Friday+Saturday = 48% of all events, indicating weekend-heavy workflow

boxes.title: Painpoints

items.name: claude-opus-4-8 used for 89% of turns — including trivial ones
items.frequency: 12,550 opus turns vs 1,201 fable turns over 39 days
items.payback: proven — cost savings compound every turn; switching 30% of trivial turns could save 3-5x on those turns
items.detail: Use /model to switch to fable-5 or sonnet for trivial tasks (explanations, quick Q&A, simple edits) and reserve opus for complex multi-tool sessions.

items.name: 26 distinct projects across 151 sessions — context sprawl
items.frequency: 26 projects; top 2 account for 119 of 151 sessions (78.8%)
items.payback: directional — focuses limited context-engineering effort on high-ROI projects
items.detail: Accept the project distribution as your actual work pattern. Focus CLAUDE.md investment on the 2 heavy-hitter projects where it compounds.

items.name: Saturday and Friday account for 48% of all events — weekend-heavy workflow
items.payback: directional — awareness-only; action depends on whether this is intentional or a crunch signal
items.detail: Observation: your weekend-heavy pattern is a descriptive finding, not a painpoint. If this is intentional, no action needed.

boxes.verdict: Opus dominance is the highest-cost habit here. The project sprawl is structural — focus context engineering where it compounds.

boxes.text: You drive sessions in a highly parallel, chain-heavy style: 94 of 151 sessions overlapped with others, 138 are chained, and the longest single-day chain hit 21 sessions on June 17. The biggest time sink is waiting — 46.7 hours idle across 852 measured turns, 47% of active wall time, with p90 wait at 8.2 minutes. You also /clear 95 times, fragmenting context and paying re-onboarding tax repeatedly. Cross-project juggling (27,078s) exceeds same-project overlap (14,992s).

boxes.note: From signals.sessions.overall and signals.sessions.responsiveness — 99.3 active hours, 46.7 hours spent waiting for agent responses

boxes.title: Wait Time by Project (hours)
boxes.note: From signals.sessions.responsiveness.by_project — route-optimization alone accounts for 24.9 of 46.7 total wait hours (53%)
From signals.sessions.parallel — cross-project juggling dominates; 36 cross-project vs 33 same-project episodes
From signals.sessions.consecutive and signals.busiest_days — 29 sessions under 1 minute likely indicate aborted or mislaunched sessions

boxes.title: Painpoints

items.name: 47% of active wall time spent waiting on the agent
items.frequency: 852 measured turns; 46.7 total wait hours across 39 days; mean 197s/turn, p90 492s/turn
items.payback: proven — 46.7 hours wasted; recovering half is 23 hours over the observation window
items.detail: Fill agent wait time with parallel manual work or backgrounded checks so you're not staring at a spinner.

items.name: 95 /clear resets — re-onboarding tax from chopped sessions
items.frequency: /clear x95, /exit x48 over 151 sessions; longest chain 21 sessions in one day (June 17, 58 hours span)
items.payback: proven — 95 clears x ~30s re-onboarding = ~4.7 hours; build cost is ~15 min of writing CLAUDE.md entries
items.detail: Move recurring setup context into CLAUDE.md so relaunches don't require re-explaining, and create a session-bootstrap skill for complex projects.

items.name: Cross-project juggling dominates parallelism — 27k vs 15k overlap seconds
items.frequency: 69 total overlap episodes; 36 cross-project, 33 same-project. 18 days with parallel sessions. Peak: 23 sessions on June 6.
items.payback: directional — hard to quantify per-instance cost, but 23-session days clearly waste re-onboarding time
items.detail: Accept cross-project juggling as your natural workflow pattern, but reduce fragmentation within each project by using fewer, longer sessions with stable context.

items.name: 29 sessions under 1 minute — aborted or mislaunched sessions
items.frequency: 29 sessions in the <1min bucket out of 151 total (19%)
items.payback: directional — 29 aborted sessions; cost per instance is small (one context load) but cumulative
items.detail: Reduce session fragmentation by being more deliberate about when to launch vs. continue an existing session.

boxes.verdict: Wait time is the single largest time sink. The /clear habit compounds it — stable context would reduce both.

boxes.text: The inner loop runs at 6.7 tool calls per turn on average, with 36% of turns being chat-only. The agent goes deep: 88 turns hit 20+ tool calls, peaking at 138 calls in a single turn. The dominant grammar is read then edit (454 transitions), followed by edit then bash verify (411). You re-steer in 9.4% of turns and interrupt 83 times — 18% of turns require correction. Explore sub-agents fire 49 times vs Plan just once, revealing no plan-first discipline.

boxes.note: From signals.loop.overall — 966 turns with 6,460 total tool calls; p90 at 18 calls/turn, max at 138
From signals.loop.cycle.tool_kind_totals — bash and edit are the dominant operations, accounting for 65% of all tool calls

boxes.title: Top Tool Transitions
boxes.note: From signals.loop.cycle.top_transitions — read->edit (454) is the primary explore-then-act pattern; edit->bash (411) is the verify-after-change path
From signals.loop.cycle and signals.loop.resteer — 494 verify-after-change events; 91 quick short restears + 83 interrupts = 174 corrections (18% of turns)
From signals.loop.leash.tool_depth_buckets — 88 turns run 20+ tool calls autonomously; longest turn had 138 calls / 395 agent steps

boxes.title: Painpoints

items.name: 9.4% of turns require re-steering — agent doing wrong thing by default
items.frequency: 91 quick short restears + 83 interrupts = 174 corrections across 966 turns (18%)
items.payback: proven — 174 corrections x ~30s each = ~8.7 hours; encoding defaults costs ~30 min one-time
items.detail: Identify the top re-steer patterns and encode them as defaults in CLAUDE.md or a skill, so the agent does the right thing without correction.

items.name: Explore sub-agent used 49x, Plan used 1x — no plan-first discipline
items.frequency: Explore x49, Plan x1 over 966 turns; 0 planning_turns in loop.cycle
items.payback: directional — 0 planning turns is a clear gap; payback depends on how many re-steers a plan would prevent
items.detail: Create a plan-first skill that the agent invokes before complex multi-file changes, producing a structured plan for your approval before executing.

items.name: edit->bash verify rate is healthy but could be automated
items.frequency: 494 verify_after_change events across 2,050 edits (24% verification rate); 411 edit->bash transitions
items.payback: directional — 76% of edits go unverified; automated checks catch errors the agent misses
items.detail: Add a post-edit hook that automatically runs a fast check (typecheck, lint, or scoped test) after the agent modifies files.

boxes.verdict: The re-steer tax (174 corrections) is the biggest loop inefficiency. A plan-first habit and encoded defaults would reduce it meaningfully.

boxes.text: You re-type the same preambles across sessions: the sources.py question 5 times, the pipeline performance target 4 times — explanations the agent re-derives from scratch each time because they live nowhere it can recall. Of 5 configured MCP servers, only 1 (gitnexus, called 2 times) was ever exercised — desktop-commander, graphify, mssql, and selenium burn context on every turn doing nothing. Your project memory files total ~8 KB, led by contract-management's CLAUDE.md at 5.3 KB.

boxes.note: From signals.context.memory_files and signals.context.mcp_surface — Jigsmith's own scaffold excluded (the tool measuring itself); 4 dead MCPs pollute context on every turn

boxes.title: Memory Files by Size (bytes)
boxes.note: From signals.context.memory_files — Jigsmith's own CLAUDE.md excluded (load-bearing scaffold, governed by tend-workbench); ~8 KB loaded across project memory files

boxes.title: Repeated Preambles (repeat count)
boxes.note: From signals.context.repeated_preambles — top 10 repeated prompts; sources.py question alone repeated 5+3=8 times across variants

boxes.title: Painpoints

items.name: Same preamble repeated 5 times — 'explain why complicated sources.py'
items.payback: proven — 8 repetitions x ~2 min each = ~16 min saved; build cost is 2 min of writing one CLAUDE.md entry
items.detail: Store the sources.py architecture rationale in the project's CLAUDE.md so the agent remembers it across sessions.

items.name: Pipeline optimization preamble repeated 4 times across sessions
items.payback: directional — 4 repetitions; thin-N but zero-cost fix
items.detail: Record the pipeline performance target and current baseline in CLAUDE.md so every new session starts with the goal.

boxes.verdict: Repeated preambles are the clearest win — store answers once in CLAUDE.md. Also worth disposing the 4 dead MCP servers (see Harness).

boxes.text: Your installed rig is lean but has dead weight: 4 of 5 MCP servers were never invoked (desktop-commander, graphify, mssql, selenium), and the Architect agent config sits unused. The only skill (textual-tui, invoked 2 times) and the only exercised MCP (gitnexus, 2 calls) show narrow actual usage. The inventory-usage join reveals 4 dead MCPs and 1 dead agent — zero-cost disposals that clean context and config.

boxes.note: From signals.harness.join and signals.harness.inventory_summary — 1 skill installed and used, 4 MCPs and 1 agent dead over 39 days

boxes.title: Installed vs Used
boxes.note: From signals.harness.join — skills are 100% utilized; MCPs are 20% (1 of 5); agents are 0% (0 of 1)

boxes.title: Painpoints

items.name: 4 MCP servers configured but never called — burning context every turn
items.frequency: 4 dead MCPs x 966 turns = 3,864 context pollutions over the observation window
items.payback: proven — zero-cost removal; recovers context tokens + attention on every turn going forward
items.detail: Disable the 4 never-exercised MCP servers to reclaim context tokens and attention on every turn.

items.name: Architect agent installed but never invoked
items.payback: directional — low per-instance cost (agent config, not MCP), but keeps the rig honest
items.detail: Remove the unused Architect agent config, or confirm it's rare-but-load-bearing and leave it.

boxes.verdict: Both are zero-cost disposals. Remove the 4 dead MCPs immediately — they burn context on every turn.

boxes.text: The shell lens is completely blind: 0 entries parsed from zsh history across the entire 39-day window. This means we can't see what you do by hand in the terminal — the very place where the highest-value forge candidates live (repeated command rituals, manual workflows). The package inventory shows 7 installed packages flagged as unused, but without shell history this is unreliable. Fixing zsh history parsing is the prerequisite for this entire lens.

boxes.note: From signals.shell.parse — complete data gap; zsh history returned 0 entries, making the entire shell lens non-functional
From signals.shell.inventory_join and signals.shell.correlation — 7 packages (cli, colima, corepack, desktop-commander, gcc, gitnexus, npm) flagged unused but unreliable without shell data

boxes.title: Painpoints

items.name: Shell history not parsed — 0 entries from zsh
items.frequency: 0 shell entries parsed — complete data gap
items.payback: proven — enables the entire shell lens; without it, forge candidates from manual work are invisible
items.detail: Fix the zsh history parsing so Jigsmith can actually see your manual work. Check that HISTFILE is set and the history file is readable.

items.name: 7 installed packages never used in shell history
items.payback: blocked — depends on shell history parsing being fixed first
items.detail: Hold on disposing these until shell history is working — the current list is unreliable without actual usage data.

boxes.verdict: Fix zsh history parsing first — everything else in this section is blocked until we can see your manual work.
